title: Letters to the Editor: The Lost Art of Composed Rage
dek: You carry your fury like a secret language, a muscle memory buried beneath years of taught shame and silencing. But what if rage could be an articulate, embodied conversation instead of a wild outburst? Here, we reclaim the lost art of composed rage—how to hold your anger in your body like a well-worn letter, written with precision, warmth, and undeniable power.
bodyMarkdown:
## Letters to the Editor: The Lost Art of Composed Rage

### The Raw Terrain of Anger in the Body

You know the feeling well, even if you’ve learned to dismiss it as something shameful, something to be quieted, hidden behind a polite smile or a polite cough. The first sign of your anger often begins as a tightening—a subtle, insistent contraction in the masseter muscle along your jawline. It’s the same muscle you clench when you’re trying not to scream, the muscle that remembers every insult, every slight you were told to swallow. That tightening spreads steadily, a slow tightening of the fascia around the jaw and cheeks, as if your whole skull is bracing for impact. Your breath shifts, shallow and quick, barely stirring the diaphragm, which itself is folding into a tight coil beneath your ribs like a startled animal. The trapezius and levator scapulae muscles, those trapezoidal and slender bands running from your neck to your shoulders, knot up with a fierce insistence, pulling those shoulders up and forward as if to shield your heart from an unseen blow.

In that moment, your body becomes a map of raw, electric tension, a living script of your anger’s message. The psoas, that deep, central muscle tucked along your lumbar spine and wrapping around the front of your hip, clenches with a primal urgency—a subtle but unyielding anchor, rooting you in the visceral truth of your experience. This is not pathology. This is biology. This is your body speaking in a language older than words.

You were never taught to listen to this language. Instead, you were taught to silence it, to smooth it over with apologies and explanations. Your fury was labeled ugly, dangerous, something to be feared or controlled. But the body remembers. Every time you swallow your rage, the masseter tightens a little more, the diaphragm flattens to a cramped, shallow breath, and the psoas tenses in a subtle but unrelenting coil. Your anger is not a failure; it is a vital messenger, carrying signals about your boundaries, your needs, your survival.

### The Quiet Power of Composed Rage

Composed rage is a lost art in this culture that prefers either the silent martyr or the eruptive volcano. It is not an absence of anger, nor is it a muted, polite version of fury. Composed rage is a deliberate, embodied presence that holds the sharp edges of anger without losing connection to the self or the body’s wisdom. It is the tension in the trapezius and levator scapulae that does not become a rigid armor but a poised readiness. It is the masseter muscle engaged but not clenched into a grimace. It is the diaphragm expanding fully, drawing in a deep, steady breath that floods the body with oxygen and steadies the inner storm.

Karla McLaren’s teachings offer a profound map for this terrain. She invites us to meet anger not as a foe but as an essential ally, a guardian of our boundaries and a clarifier of our truth. In her work, anger is not the enemy of peace but the herald of it—when attended with care and curiosity. McLaren teaches that anger has a precise somatic signature, that it carries specific energies and messages that must be understood on the level of the body, not just the mind.

To embody composed rage, you learn to observe the subtle contractions in your psoas, the gentle flare and release of the diaphragm, the modulation of tension in the trapezius. You cultivate a somatic literacy, a listening practice that honors the body’s signals without letting them run unchecked. This is not suppression but a dynamic containment, a holding pattern that respects the power of your rage without allowing it to consume or fragment you.

### The Jaw Remembers: Anger as a Somatic Script

There is a reason the jaw remembers, why the masseter muscle holds onto grudges long after the mind has tried to forget. Anger, stored here, is a story etched into muscle and fascia, a chronic tension that can make your face ache on a rainy afternoon or wake you in the small hours of the morning. You might not have realized that your levator scapulae—the small muscles at the back of your neck that help tilt your head—carry a heavy part of your anger’s weight. When these muscles are chronically tight, your head feels like it’s being pulled to one side, a physical metaphor for the imbalance anger creates in your nervous system.

The diaphragm, that large, dome-shaped muscle beneath your lungs, is central to how anger moves through you. When you feel rage rising, the diaphragm often tightens prematurely, cutting off the deep, grounding breath that could help you stay present. Instead, your breathing becomes erratic, caught in the upper chest, causing a sense of panic or overwhelm. In the practice of composed rage, you train this muscle to stay open, to breathe fully and steadily even as the fire of anger burns within.

The psoas, often overlooked, is a key player here. This deep hip flexor muscle is sometimes called the “muscle of the soul” because of its intimate connection to our core sense of safety and presence. When anger is unexpressed, the psoas tends to tighten, pulling the lumbar spine into a protective curl that can feel like shrinking into yourself. But when you embrace composed rage, you allow the psoas to soften just enough to support standing in your power, spine elongated and stable, rooted in your body’s wisdom.

Through McLaren’s lens, anger is neither villain nor victim. It is a precise force with a message, a somatic script waiting to be read and honored. To recover the art of composed rage is to reclaim this story, to learn to hold the body’s tension and release it with intention, to listen deeply to the muscles and fascia that have carried your fury for far too long. This is not a quick fix or a feel-good platitude. It is a somatic revolution, a return to the body’s ancient intelligence that knows how to hold rage without breaking.

## Herbal Allies for the Fierce Pulse of Anger

You sit into the heat of your own rage, chest tightening like a fist around your heart, the masseter muscle in your jaw clenching as if it’s trying to grind the moment into ash. This is not pathology; this is biology speaking in the rawest dialect. Your body remembers every time you were told to swallow your fury, to make it pretty, polite, or disappear altogether. But the body does not lie. It knows. It stores. And it demands medicine that meets it where it is, fierce and unflinching.

Enter the realm of herbal allies—the plants that have been speaking to the body’s fire for millennia, their chemistry as rich and precise as any pharmaceutical concoction, but with a softness that invites you to witness your rage rather than suppress it. The bitterness of **Bupleurum** root, a staple in Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM), courses through your liver meridian where anger tends to stick and stagnate like thick molasses in a cold jar. Bupleurum’s slightly cooling energy moves qi—the vital life force—like a river breaking through a dam, unblocking the stuck tension that curls along your trapezius and levator scapulae muscles, those silent sentinels braced against unseen enemies.

In the same TCM pharmacy of rage, **Chai Hu** (another name for Bupleurum) partners with **Xiang Fu** (Cyperus rotundus), a rhizome that smells faintly of earth after rain and carries the deep wisdom of the psoas muscle’s twisting coil—the muscle that holds your deepest fears and furious “no’s” in its grasp. Together, these herbs untangle that visceral knot, not by dulling your edge but by refining it, turning the raw flame of anger into a controlled forge.

You might also reach for **Skullcap** (Scutellaria lateriflora), a nervine herb that’s not about sedation but about clarity—like a cool hand on the diaphragm where rage often pins you mid-breath. It’s the difference between stifled fury and composed rage. Skullcap’s subtle bitterness opens the fascia around your ribcage, where the diaphragm’s relentless contraction screams for space to exhale your truth.

For the night when the anger wakes you at 3 a.m., hot and unrelenting, and the muscles along your jaw and neck tighten into a silent scream, a tincture of **Passionflower** (Passiflora incarnata) leans in with a lullaby of magnesium-rich calm. It doesn’t erase your fire; it quiets the tempest enough so you can hear what your body is saying beneath the racket.

These are not remedies for weakness or quietness, but for the fierce, unyielding presence of anger that refuses to be shamed away. They honor the body’s language and invite a conversation, not a command. As Karla McLaren insists, anger is a messenger with a very specific vocabulary—herbs like these translate that message into a language your muscles can understand.

## Embodied Practice: The Jaw’s Quiet Rebellion

You wake sometime in the deep dark before dawn, the sky a bruise of indigo and cloud. The masseter muscle clenches reflexively, as if your body is still holding onto last night’s fight, or perhaps a fight that never happened but lived in your nervous system anyway. You can almost smell the faint musk of your own skin, warmed by restless sleep, the slight salt of your breath mingling with the faint scent of chamomile on your pillowcase.

Here lies the lost art of composed rage: a practice not of silencing fury but of letting it move through the body with precision. You place your fingertips just where your jaw hinges, feeling the tautness in the masseter and temporalis muscles. The skin feels warm, almost pulsing with unspent energy. This is your first contact with anger’s physical footprint.

Begin by gently pressing into the fibers, coaxing them to release the clenched memory. The pressure is deliberate but tender, not to crush but to negotiate. You open your mouth slowly, like a gate swinging on ancient hinges, and then close it with mindful softness. The jaw remembers every time it was told to shut up, to bite down hard and keep the fury inside. Your touch says: here is space to breathe, to unfold.

From here, you move to the shoulders—those raised bastions of defense, the trapezius and levator scapulae taut from a lifetime of bracing against unseen threats. You imagine the tight bands of muscle as ropes fraying in the rain, the fascia loosening its grip. You inhale deeply into your diaphragm, the breath expanding your ribcage like a bellows, and on the exhale, you let your shoulders drop with the slow inevitability of gravity.

Resmaa Menakem’s teachings echo here with profound clarity: “The body keeps the score, but it also keeps the peace.” He reminds us that racialized trauma, intergenerational rage, and personal fury are stored in the body’s tissues—the skin, the muscles, the very marrow of our bones. To heal, you must become a somatic witness to your own rage, to feel it coursing through your blood and fascia without flinching.

You lie down on the cool floor, the slight chill against your skin a sharp counterpoint to the heat rising in your chest. Place your hands on your belly, right over the psoas muscle, the deep core muscle that curls like a coiled serpent beneath the abdomen. It tightens when anger strikes, preparing your body to flee or fight. Instead of fighting that coil, you breathe into it, imagining your breath as a slow tide that ebbs and flows through that muscle’s grasp.

This is not a passive practice but an active engagement with your rage’s physical form. It’s a negotiation with the body’s memory, not an erasure. The body knows when it is respected.

## The Letters We Write With Our Bodies

In the era of instant reactions and digital outbursts, the lost art of composed rage feels like a relic—an elegant letter penned in the ink of muscle tension and breath rather than typed in the heat of a keyboard. But this kind of letter is written in the body’s language, in the subtle contractions of the diaphragm and the slow unwinding of the masseter. It is a dialogue with your own nervous system, a message to the muscles that have held your fury so long they have forgotten how to let go.

When you engage with your anger this way, you become a letter-writer to yourself, crafting communication that is deliberate, precise, and deeply embodied. The herbs you take are the stationery—Bupleurum’s bitter tang, Skullcap’s cool caress—while the somatic practice is the ink flowing onto the page, each breath and muscle release a carefully chosen word.

Resmaa Menakem’s work on racialized trauma teaches that these letters are not simply personal but political, woven into the collective body’s history. Your composed rage becomes a radical act of self-preservation and resistance, a refusal to allow your fury to be weaponized against you by shame or silence.

In the quiet hours when the world sleeps and your body hums with the residue of rage, you learn to listen deeply to the letters your muscles write. The jaw remembers every insult, every dismissal, but it also remembers how to unclench, how to open. The psoas remembers every fight-or-flight impulse, but it also remembers the slow tide of breath that can calm it. The trapezius remembers every burden you carried, but it also remembers how to let go.

This is the lost art of composed rage—an ancient conversation between herbs, breath, muscle, and memory. It is not about controlling anger but about honoring its message, its medicine, and its presence in the body’s living script. It is, above all, a letter to yourself: You are seen. You are heard. Your fury is not ugly. It is essential.

## The Resonance of Composed Rage in the Body’s Architecture

You’ve felt it, haven’t you? That slow burn behind the eyes, the tightening around the masseter muscles, the way your trapezius stiffens like a drawn bowstring as you prepare to speak with measured fury. This is not some abstract emotional state but a vivid, somatic choreography your body performs when rage is held with intention rather than suppressed or exploded. The art of composed rage is a somatic language that the body knows deeply. It is a muscle memory of resistance, an embodied refusal to dissolve into impulsivity or silence.

The body’s fascia—those silky sheets enveloping every muscle and organ—remembers how to hold rage without retraumatizing itself. When you breathe into the diaphragm, letting the breath be slow and full, you invite a calm that doesn’t dilute the fire but contains it, like a carefully tended hearth. This is the wisdom of teachers like Thich Nhat Hanh, who so often reminds us to “embrace anger with mindfulness,” and Karla McLaren, whose work insists that anger is a vital, clarifying force when held with respect. It’s not about quelling the flame but about learning to wield it, to channel that heat into crisp, articulate expression.

You might notice the levator scapulae—the tendons that run from your neck to your shoulder blades—throbbing in that moment of restrained fury. It is the physical echo of your refusal to be diminished, your body’s way of setting boundaries without collapsing. Composed rage is the sharp edge of self-preservation; it’s the voice that rises from your core, not from a place of moral failure, but from a biology finely tuned to protect and proclaim. This is the lost art, the practice that turns a scream into a clear, unshakeable statement of truth.

## The Alchemy of Anger and Healing

When you practice composed rage, you are engaging in a profound alchemy. You are taking the raw material of your fury—the heat in your chest, the tension in your jaw, the rapid pulse of your heart—and transforming it into a tool for healing. This is not a passive process. It requires your full presence, your willingness to sit with the discomfort of your own anger without rushing to fix it or push it away.

Gabor Maté’s work on the mind-body connection reminds us that suppressed anger is a potent source of illness and dis-ease. When we swallow our rage, we are swallowing a poison that slowly erodes our vitality. But when we allow our anger to move through us, when we give it space to breathe and speak, we are engaging in a radical act of self-care. We are telling our bodies that they are safe, that their signals are valid, and that their wisdom is honored.

This alchemy is supported by the herbal allies we invite into our practice. The bitter roots of Bupleurum, the cooling touch of Skullcap, the gentle embrace of Passionflower—these are not just remedies; they are partners in our healing. They help to soften the edges of our rage, to untangle the knots in our fascia, and to soothe the frayed nerves that have carried the weight of our anger for so long.

As you sit with your composed rage, you may find that it begins to shift. The sharp, jagged edges may soften into a deep, resonant ache. The heat may cool into a steady, grounding warmth. This is the alchemy at work. Your anger is not disappearing; it is integrating. It is becoming a part of your strength, your resilience, your capacity to stand firmly in your truth.

## The Legacy of Composed Rage

The lost art of composed rage is not just a personal practice; it is a legacy we pass down to the next generation. When we learn to hold our anger with intention and care, we are breaking the cycle of shame and silence that has been handed down to us. We are teaching our children, our communities, and our world that anger is not a monster to be feared, but a messenger to be respected.

This legacy is built on the teachings of those who have walked this path before us—teachers like Thich Nhat Hanh, Karla McLaren, Resmaa Menakem, and Gabor Maté. Their wisdom provides the foundation for our practice, the map that guides us through the raw terrain of our own fury. But the journey is ours to make. It is our bodies that must learn the language of composed rage, our muscles that must remember how to hold the tension without breaking.

As you move through the world with your composed rage, you become a living testament to the power of this practice. Your presence is a quiet rebellion against a culture that demands compliance and silence. Your voice, steady and clear, is a beacon for others who are struggling to find their own.

The lost art of composed rage is a gift you give to yourself, and a gift you offer to the world. It is the courage to feel deeply, to speak truthfully, and to stand unapologetically in the full power of your own humanity.

---

**Q: Is composed rage safe to practice in all situations?**  
**A:** Safety is paramount. Composed rage is an internal state that you cultivate before external expression. It requires you to be attuned to your body’s signals and to choose the appropriate context for expression. In environments where speaking out might provoke harm, practicing composed rage might look like journaling, movement, or somatic release in private spaces until you feel ready. The goal is never to provoke violence but to reclaim your agency. As Bessel van der Kolk teaches, trauma-informed practice respects the nervous system’s limits—composed rage honors that by inviting you to build resilience gradually.

**Q: Can composed rage help with healing trauma?**  
**A:** Absolutely. Trauma often manifests as frozen or unexpressed rage trapped in the body’s fascia and musculature. The psoas, masseter, and trapezius muscles can hold tension from past violations, and composed rage offers a way to mobilize this energy safely. Pema Chödrön’s teachings on sitting with discomfort and Gabor Maté’s work on embodied trauma converge here: when you allow yourself to feel anger fully and hold it without judgment, you interrupt the cycle of repression and shame. This embodied practice can release stored trauma and foster integration, turning rage into a source of personal power rather than pain.

**Q: How does composed rage relate to cultural and gendered expectations around anger?**  
**A:** Women, in particular, have been socialized to see anger as “ugly” or “unfeminine,” leading to deep bodily shame and suppression. This cultural conditioning affects the body’s ability to hold rage without dissociation or outburst. Composed rage is a radical act of resistance against these norms. It restores dignity to your fury by honoring its role as a boundary-setter and survival mechanism. Resmaa Menakem’s work on racialized trauma and the body underscores how societal forces shape how anger is embodied across different bodies. Reclaiming composed rage is reclaiming your right to inhabit your body fully, with all its fierce beauty intact.

**Q: What practical steps can I take to begin cultivating composed rage?**  
**A:** Start by bringing mindful awareness to your anger’s physical sensations. Set aside time—perhaps late at night when the world is quiet and your diaphragm can expand fully—to breathe deeply into the areas where you feel tension. Use gentle movement to release the trapezius or roll your shoulders to ease the levator scapulae. Write letters to your “editor”—your inner critic, or the world—without editing yourself. Infuse your practice with herbal allies chosen for their calming or grounding properties. Seek out somatic guides or teachers who specialize in trauma-informed anger work, like Robert Augustus Masters or Karla McLaren. Most importantly, practice patience. The body remembers, but it does so in its own time.

**Q: How do I know if I’m practicing composed rage effectively?**  
**A:** You’ll notice a difference in how your body responds. Instead of the tight, chaotic clenching of muscles or the overwhelming surge that leaves you drained, you’ll find a steady, sustained energy—the masseter muscles engaged but not clenched; the diaphragm full and open; the shoulders relaxed yet alert. Emotionally, you’ll feel clearer, less reactive, more in command of your voice in difficult conversations. Your letters, your words, will carry a sharpness tempered by calm, a message delivered with the force of a well-honed arrow rather than a wild storm. If frustration or overwhelm arises, return to your breath and your body, honoring where you are without self-judgment.

**Q: Can composed rage coexist with compassion?**  
**A:** Yes. Composed rage is not about hardening yourself or turning cold. It is a fierce tenderness—a recognition that your anger is a signal of unmet needs and boundaries, not a moral failing. This tenderness extends outward, allowing you to hold space for others’ imperfections while still maintaining your own integrity. Teachers like Thich Nhat Hanh and Pema Chödrön have long shown us that true compassion includes the courage to stand firmly in our truth. Composed rage is that courage made flesh, a way of saying no without losing your heart.

In reclaiming composed rage, you reclaim your body’s voice—the whispered hum of the diaphragm, the steady pulse of the psoas, the resolute line of the jaw. This is not just an emotional practice; it is a return to your body’s deep, unyielding wisdom. Your rage, held with care, is a testament to your survival and your power. It is time to write your letter. It is time to speak with the full weight of your body behind your words.
